scope: A cross-tradition directory of Christian leaders, writers, researchers, pastors, journalists, and organizations whose first-party work explicitly challenges Christian nationalism or the political capture of Christianity.
editorial_note: Inclusion documents relevant public work; it does not imply affiliation with TDS, endorsement of this site, or agreement among the people and organizations listed.

movements.name: Christians Against Christian Nationalism
movements.description: A BJC-led grassroots campaign, launched in 2019, that equips Christians and allies to recognize and resist Christian nationalism in churches, communities, and public life.

movements.name: Faithful America
movements.description: A national, ecumenical online community organizing Christians against Christian nationalism, white supremacy, and the use of faith to rationalize injustice.

movements.name: Vote Common Good
movements.description: A Christian-led civic organization offering a free, three-session curriculum for understanding Christian nationalism and responding without surrendering love of neighbor.

movements.name: Disarming Leviathan
movements.description: Pastor Caleb Campbell’s ministry helps people engage Christian nationalists in families and churches with clarity, hospitality, and practical gospel-centered tools.

movements.name: Repairers of the Breach
movements.description: Bishop William J. Barber II’s organization develops moral leaders and public action rooted in the prophetic traditions of faith, including direct opposition to religious nationalism.

movements.name: Red Letter Christians
movements.description: A network centering the teachings of Jesus and challenging nationalism, militarism, racism, and other political ideologies presented as Christianity.

voices.name: Amanda Tyler
voices.description: Executive director of BJC, lead organizer of Christians Against Christian Nationalism, co-host of Respecting Religion, and author of How to End Christian Nationalism.
voices.why: Start here for church-state expertise, organizing strategy, and a direct Christian case against merging faith with national identity.

voices.name: William J. Barber II
voices.description: Bishop and president of Repairers of the Breach, known for building a moral movement around poverty, democracy, racism, and the public responsibilities of faith.
voices.why: Follow for Black church prophetic witness that rejects religious nationalism and connects public morality to the people policy harms first.

voices.name: Russell Moore
voices.description: Christianity Today editor at large and columnist writing about moral courage, Christian ethics, and putting the gospel ahead of politics and tribal loyalty.
voices.why: Follow for an evangelical critique of political idolatry addressed to readers still inside conservative Christian institutions.

voices.name: Jemar Tisby
voices.description: A historian of race and religion whose work traces how white supremacy, Christian institutions, and Christian nationalism reinforce one another.
voices.why: Follow for historically grounded analysis that treats racial justice as central—not incidental—to Christian public witness.

voices.name: Kristin Kobes Du Mez
voices.description: A Calvin University historian whose research examines the intersection of gender, religion, politics, militant masculinity, and evangelical support for authoritarian power.
voices.why: Follow for the historical architecture behind the Trump-era alliance between white evangelical identity and strongman politics.

voices.name: Andrew Whitehead
voices.description: A sociologist of religion and author of American Idolatry, which argues that Christian nationalism betrays the gospel and damages the church.
voices.why: Follow for empirical research translated into language congregations and ordinary Christians can use.

voices.name: Caleb Campbell
voices.description: Lead pastor of Desert Springs Bible Church and founder of Disarming Leviathan, a ministry for people trying to reach Christian nationalists close to home.
voices.why: Follow for practical, gentle, non-naive ways to hold truth and relationship together in a church or family conversation.

voices.name: Shane Claiborne
voices.description: A Red Letter Christian who measures public faith against the words and life of Jesus, with sustained critiques of nationalism, violence, and political power clothed in Christian symbols.
voices.why: Follow for accessible, movement-minded writing that keeps returning the argument to Jesus and the Sermon on the Mount.

voices.name: Tim Whitaker
voices.description: Founder of The New Evangelicals, a media and community project confronting Christian nationalism, harmful church systems, and exclusionary evangelical politics.
voices.why: Follow for frank interviews, social-first explainers, and a community built for people reconstructing faith after evangelical disillusionment.

voices.name: Skye Jethani
voices.description: A pastor and writer who co-hosts Holy Post, a Christian media platform that regularly disentangles discipleship from partisan identity and explains Christian nationalism for church audiences.
voices.why: Follow for thoughtful, conversational analysis that can travel well in an evangelical small group or family discussion.

voices.name: Jim Wallis
voices.description: Theologian, teacher, and Sojourners founder whose recent work calls Christians to reject white Christian nationalism and recover a multiracial, democracy-protecting public faith.
voices.why: Follow for a long-view social-gospel argument connecting faith, race, democracy, and public responsibility.

voices.name: Brian Kaylor
voices.description: President and editor in chief of Word&Way, host of Dangerous Dogma, and author of The Bible According to Christian Nationalists.
voices.why: Follow for reported analysis of how scripture is used—and misused—in contemporary Christian nationalist politics.
